text.title: Alert Triage Decision Tree Core Rules
text.summary: 7 testable rules with rationale + source for alert-triage-decision-tree. Includes terminal skip-this-methodology rule.

rule.statement: Severity MUST be derived from observable signals (error rate threshold, saturation ratio, customer-impact proxy), not from the alert's hardcoded label.
rule.rationale: SRE workbook (Beyer et al, 2018) — hardcoded severity drifts; signal-derived severity tracks reality.

rule.statement: Triage MUST classify blast radius (single-user / single-tenant / cohort / global); blast radius defines escalation, not severity alone.
rule.rationale: Google SRE error-budget model — a high-severity alert affecting one tenant routes differently from a low-severity alert affecting all.

rule.statement: Alerts older than 5 minutes MUST be cross-checked against current signal before paging — most stale alerts self-heal.
rule.rationale: PagerDuty 2024 noise report — 38% of stale alerts self-resolve before responder arrival.

rule.statement: Every route MUST name an owner (team handle or rotation), never 'on-call' as opaque pointer.
rule.rationale: Atlassian incident management — opaque on-call leads to drop-zone alerts.

rule.statement: Auto-suppression MUST emit an audit entry with rule_id + alert payload + suppression rationale; suppression is reviewed weekly.
rule.rationale: OPS-Trust report 2025 — silent suppression is the leading cause of missed P0s.

rule.statement: If route SLA is not acknowledged within window, escalation MUST page the next tier (manager-on-call), recorded in the audit log.
rule.rationale: PagerDuty escalation policy primitives — silent SLA breach corrupts trust in the pager.

rule.statement: If team has no paging surface or alerts are staging-only, return `skipped`.
